text: Baichuan is located on the southern slope of the Sumenshan Mountain in the northwest of Huixian City, Xinxiang. From the records in "Shuijingzhu" to the Republican era, it has undertaken the dual historical missions of navigation and irrigation. In the early days of liberation, its spring water still amounted to 1.23×10^8 m³/a, serving as an important source of water supply for industrial and agricultural use in Huixian City. Baichuan is renowned both domestically and internationally for its beautiful waters and mountains, as well as its historical sites, earning it the titles of "Summer Palace of Zhongzhou" and "Little West Lake of the North." However, after 1979, due to climate change and unreasonable human exploitation, the spring water of Baichuan has long dried up (only briefly flowing during the wet season in years of abundant rainfall), leading to ecological degradation that has severely impacted the high-quality development of tourism and related industries in Xinxiang. To restore the former beauty of Baichuan, the Huixian People's Government has conducted two large-scale "water restoration projects" in 1982 and 2018, which included measures such as sealing leaks at the bottom of Baichuan Lake, enhancing fountain lighting, paving roads, and greening the land. Although Baichuan has temporarily ended its "waterless mode," the restoration of the once-splendid scenery of "a hundred springs gushing" and "pearls and jade spouting" remains a distant hope.

The flow of the Baiquan spring is not only constrained by climate change but is also closely related to human extraction activities. 1964-1972: The extraction of karst water in the Baiquan spring area was almost nonexistent; even during the severe drought of 1965 (with an annual precipitation of only 340mm), the Baiquan spring did not dry up. 1972-1979: In the early stages of karst water extraction, the number of karst water wells increased from just a few to 55. The Yugong Spring and the Rose Spring also established pumping stations, and in May 1979, the Baiquan spring experienced a cessation of flow. 1980-2004: During the period of increased karst water extraction, with advancements in exploration well technology and a continuous rise in water resource demand, the first water source of the Huixian City Water Company, the water source of the Huixian City Power Plant, the Xinxiang Fengquan Water Plant, and various township enterprises began to extract large amounts of karst water from the spring area. The Baiquan spring would only flow during the wet season of years with abundant rainfall.

2005-2020: Peak period of karst water extraction. With the increasing demand for water resources and advancements in exploration well technology, the second water plant in Huixian City began operations, and the Fengquan Water Plant expanded its extraction. Notably, a large number of deep karst wells were drilled in the Baichuan spring area (around Mengzhuang South and Chenbao), with depths generally ranging from 500 to 1000 meters, and some reaching up to 2500 meters. As shown in Table 5-1, the number of karst water extraction wells in the spring area reached 300 in 2011, and by 2021, it exceeded a thousand, continuing to increase by dozens each year. The rapid increase in both the volume of karst water extraction and the number of extraction wells has led to a continuous decline in the groundwater level within the spring area, which can no longer reach the outflow level of Baichuan spring, resulting in long-term drying up of the spring water.

In summary, the cessation of spring water at Baiquan is due to the improvement in exploration and drilling technology, the continuous increase in the depth and number of extraction wells, and the growing volume of karst water extraction. Before 1978, Baiquan had a year-round natural spring flow; after 1979, with the rapid development of industry and agriculture, the establishment of karst water wells, karst water sources, and irrigation stations led to a sharp increase in the extraction of karst water, resulting in a continuous decline in the underground water level and prolonged drying of the spring water.

In the Baichuan spring storage area, including Mengzhuang Town, Baichuan Town, Beiyunmen Town, and Huqiao Street in Huixian City, as well as Genghuang Township and Dakua Town in the scenic area, the ground elevation is all lower than the elevation of the Baichuan spring outlet (+85.0m). The depths of the wells extracting fissure karst water mostly range from 300 to 600 meters, with some reaching over a thousand meters. There are more than 700 wells in total, and after the resurgence of Baichuan, all these wells flow freely (even after Baichuan dries up, some wells still flow freely). According to on-site investigations, after the heavy rainstorm in Huixian on July 22, the resurgence of Baichuan resulted in a maximum free flow rate of spring water reaching 10m³/s. A large amount of karst water was discharged into nearby ditches through the unsealed free-flowing wells, with some flowing into nearby farmland, causing the fields to become marshy, leading to water resource wastage and land resource damage.

The cessation of the Baiquan spring flow is attributed to the advancements in exploration and drilling technology, the continuous increase in the depth and number of extraction wells, and the growing volume of karst water extraction. From 1979 to 2004, the contribution of climate change to the depletion of the spring water was 1.063 m³/s, while human extraction activities contributed 2.572 m³/s. From 2005 to 2020, the contribution of climate change to the depletion of the spring water was 1.023 m³/s, and human extraction activities contributed 2.881 m³/s.

Taking Jinan, a national historical and cultural city, as an example, since the 1970s, the spring water resources within the Jinan area have experienced several instances of seasonal drying up and even consecutive years of drought. In recent years, under the unified organization of the local government, a series of measures such as ecological restoration of spring water recharge areas, water injection to maintain springs, and well sealing to protect springs have been implemented, resulting in the continuous flow of the main spring groups in the urban area since their resurgence on September 6, 2003. However, this success in protecting the springs at a purely natural material level cannot halt the gradual decline and eventual disappearance of the traditional environmental characteristics of spring water settlements. Against the backdrop of excessive urban development in our country, many historical districts have been demolished, and the newly constructed traditional-style buildings lack the natural integration with the spring water landscape, thereby losing the "core" qualities of the spring water settlement environment.

Baiquan in Xingtai, Hebei, is located within a 20-square-kilometer area on both sides of the Jingguang Railway in Xingtai County. With countless springs, it is aptly named "Baiquan," featuring 15 spring groups including Baiquan, Pearl Spring, Han Family Spring, Silver Sand Spring, and Dahuo Spring. According to the stone tablet at the Longwang Temple of Baiquan, as early as the Hongzhi period of the Ming Dynasty, local residents began to dredge canals and excavate riverbeds, channeling the waters of Baiquan to irrigate farmland. The "Xingtai County Chronicle" records that downstream of Baiquan River, near the southern city of He County, there were thirteen river channels built, and the abundant spring water resources along with developed water conservancy facilities led to the emergence of several village settlements around the numerous springs. However, in recent decades, the once vibrant scene of "water gushing from a hundred holes, sweet dew overflowing" has disappeared.

Additionally, historical records indicate that Beijing was once rich in springs. During the Song, Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties, areas such as Zizhuyuan, Diaoyutai, Nanyuan, Caoqiao, Tuanbo, and Zhangjiawan in Tongzhou were abundant in spring water resources. The royal gardens in the inner city were built around these springs, and villages and towns sprang up in the suburbs, thriving on the water sources, earning the title of a true "Spring City." According to a citywide survey conducted from 1979 to 1980, there were 1,246 springs with measurable flow during the dry season. This number exceeds the total number of springs in Jinan, known as the Spring City, by more than ten times. However, due to the extraction of groundwater in Beijing far exceeding its replenishment, most springs have long since dried up, and contemporary Beijing has completely lost the characteristic features of its spring settlements.

Dayangquan Village is surrounded by mountains on three sides, with water to the south. The village is located on the northern slope of the water, and its overall layout aligns well with the ideal feng shui patterns based on traditional feng shui principles. … Yangquan City boasts excellent geographical conditions, with rich mineral resources underground. The black (coal), white (bauxite), and yellow (pyrite) are known as the "Three Treasures of Yangquan." Yangquan's anthracite coal is renowned nationwide, and it is the extraction of coal resources that has led to a sharp decline in water levels in the Yangquan area, causing the main spring in the village, Shanggang, to dry up as early as the 1970s.

The current crisis of spring water settlements is primarily due to the objective factor of increasingly depleted water resources, while the transformation of the value of spring water within settlements under industrial civilization serves as the main internal cause of their predicament. This paper comprehensively summarizes the real issues faced by spring water settlements today, explores the new resource and cultural values of spring water in the industrial era, and engages in strategic discussions on the sustainable use of spring water resources, the organic renewal of settlement architecture, and the preservation of the spring water spatial environment.

The karst groundwater system in the Jinan Spring area is highly water-rich, and its abundant groundwater resources provide support for social development. However, during annual dry periods, the spring water is still threatened by interruptions or short-term interruptions, primarily due to a lack of clear understanding of the karst water system in the Jinan Spring area. Therefore, this paper focuses on the karst water system in the Jinan Spring area. Based on an investigation of the hydrogeological conditions in the Jinan Spring area, it employs monitoring of spring and groundwater levels and water quality, tracer tests, tritium isotope testing, and numerical simulation of groundwater to identify key factors from three aspects: the groundwater dynamic field, hydrochemical field, and temperature field. It classifies different levels of flow systems and reveals the mechanisms by which these different levels of flow systems affect spring water levels, water quality, and water quantity.

The karst water system in northern China is highly water-rich, and its abundant groundwater resources provide support for social development. The Jinan spring group, as a typical representative of large northern karst springs, serves as an important carrier of both cultural and natural landscapes, and is extensively used for industrial production, agricultural irrigation, and residents' daily life, thus providing strong support for the economic and social development of Jinan. However, since the 1970s, the extensive extraction of groundwater in the spring area has led to spring water interruption. Although artificial recharge has been carried out in the recharge area of the spring group since 2003, the spring water still faces the threat of interruption during dry periods. The high degree of heterogeneous anisotropy in the northern karst aquifer media results in extremely complex karst hydraulic fields, temperature fields, and hydrochemical fields. Multiple tracer tests have shown that the macroscopic average velocity of flow in the Jinan spring area's karst water system varies greatly, with uneven distribution of karst fractures, the presence of flow systems at different levels, and the vertical zonation of karst development leading to differences between the macroscopically unified hydraulic connections and the local hydraulic connections.

Meng Qingbin's study on the relationship between the "Three Waters" transformation and spring water restoration in the Jinan spring area analyzes the dynamic characteristics of Jinan's spring water. It categorizes the transformation patterns of the karst water system's "Three Waters" in the Jinan spring area. The research indicates that the fundamental reasons for the degradation of infiltration functions in the groundwater recharge area and the interruption of spring flow are the reduction of river runoff recharge in the indirect recharge area, the decreased infiltration capacity of river channels in the direct recharge area, and urbanization. Meng Qingling and others employed time series analysis to examine the response characteristics of different groundwater flows in the Jinan spring area to precipitation. The study shows that atmospheric precipitation remains the only and most significant factor affecting spring flow in the spring season. Through correlation analysis and spectral analysis, they calculated the time-lag response of different types of groundwater flow to precipitation. However, current research on spring water levels and flow is still limited to simple analyses of data time series, and the hydrological and geological information implied by spring dynamics has not been thoroughly explored. There is also a lack of in-depth understanding and research on the impact of multi-level flow systems on the dynamics of karst springs.

With the development of the economy, the pace of urbanization has accelerated, leading to advancements in transportation and an increase in housing. This has resulted in a gradual reduction of the recharge area within the spring region, along with some surface hardening, which affects the sources of recharge for the springs. During the annual dry seasons, the springs continue to face threats of interruption or short-term cessation. Therefore, by delineating different hierarchical flow systems within the Jinan spring region and calculating the recharge proportions of these various systems, we can reveal the mechanisms through which different flow systems influence the dynamics of the springs. This is of significant importance for understanding the formation process of seasonal springs in the Jinan spring region and provides crucial guidance for spring protection efforts and the formulation of water supply plans for spring conservation.

The Quanshui River basin is characterized by a subtropical monsoon climate, with an average annual rainfall ranging from 900 to 1100 mm. However, the distribution of rainfall throughout the seasons is highly uneven, with a concentration occurring from June to August. This leads to significant seasonal variations in surface water and groundwater dynamics. From August to October 2018, the study area experienced persistent drought, with only 35 mm of rainfall recorded (according to Rainlog), while the Jing Shan meteorological station recorded 97.4 mm during the same period. This indicates a significant deficit in precipitation, which may have impacted the runoff of the Quanshui River and the emergence of spring water.

The groundwater in Pine Spring River Basin is primarily replenished by atmospheric precipitation. Springs are predominantly located at the junctions of rock layers and near faults, but the flow rate of these springs is generally low (<5 tons/day), indicating a limited discharge of spring water. During dry seasons or years with below-average precipitation, the flow rate of spring water may further decrease. The electrical conductivity (EC) of surface water shows a spatial pattern where the upper and middle reaches are higher than the lower reaches, particularly at sampling point R6, where EC values significantly drop during the wet season and then rise again, possibly related to river dam regulation and seasonal fluctuations in water volume. In October 2019, with very little rainfall, surface water primarily relied on groundwater and spring water for replenishment, highlighting the importance of spring water in maintaining surface water under drought conditions.

In October 2018, the rainfall was only 9.9 mm. The concentration changes of surface water NO₃⁻ and Cl⁻ ions were mainly influenced by groundwater and spring water recharge, indicating that during this dry period, spring water and groundwater became the primary sources of surface water. This also suggests that surface runoff was almost stagnant, with spring water discharge being crucial for maintaining river flow. In the high-frequency monitoring conducted during the day and night in winter and summer, the flow rates at the TW section were 161 m³/d (winter) and 1949 m³/d (summer), respectively. The summer flow rate was significantly greater than that of winter, indicating a substantial seasonal difference in spring water flow, with a notable reduction in water volume during winter. Although there was no complete cessation of flow, it was close to a low-flow state.

Springs, as one of the important discharge methods of groundwater, are not only a vital freshwater resource but also a significant source of high-quality drinking water for agricultural and industrial production, as well as for residents. Globally, many springs serve as important tourist attractions and cultural heritage sites, such as the Grand Prismatic Spring in the United States, Arima Onsen in Japan, the Blue Lagoon in Iceland, and the Qiong Spring and Tianchi Lake in Changbai Mountain in China. However, under the dual impacts of climate change and human activities, many springs have experienced phenomena such as flow interruption or drying up. For instance, some spring outlets in the Heilangtan Spring Group in Lijiang, Yunnan Province, have frequently experienced flow interruptions since 2006, and the Water God Hall Spring in Guangling County, Shanxi Province, experienced flow interruption in July 2014. The interruption of spring flow not only causes economic losses in regions that rely on springs as tourist resources but also leads to issues such as regional water resource shortages, deteriorating water quality, and worsening ecological environments, raising significant concerns among governments and scholars. Therefore, conducting research on the recharge sources, circulation paths, recharge cycles of springs, and their responses to climate change is essential for the rational utilization of spring water resources. This research holds significant guiding and promoting significance for the scientific management of regional water resources and the sustainable development of spring area tourism.

Jinan, Shandong is renowned worldwide for its springs and is honored with the title of "Spring City." It boasts three major spring areas and 72 famous springs, including the Tuquan Spring Area, the Baiquan Spring Area, and the Mingshui Spring Area (also known as the Baima Spring). Among the 72 famous springs are the Qiong Spring, the Baima Spring, the Shuyu Spring, and the Pearl Spring, among others. The Baima Spring group, part of the Mingshui Spring Area, is located in the Zhangqiao District of Jinan, Shandong, and is the former residence of Li Qingzhao, a renowned female poet from the Song Dynasty, often regarded as "the greatest female talent of all time" in China. In 2005, it was awarded the title of "Global Excellent Ecotourism Destination" by the United Nations Committee for International Cooperation and Coordination (CCC/UN) at the Global Forum on Human Settlements. In 2006, it became a service brand of Shandong Province and a national AAAA-level tourist attraction. However, in recent years, increased groundwater extraction and the development of the mining industry have led to intermittent or seasonal drying up of many springs in the area. Statistics show that the Baima Spring experienced 70 days of drying up in 1993; 527 days from 1999 to 2000; 909 days from 2001 to 2003; and 740 days from 2014 to 2016. This drying up has not only had a series of impacts on the regional ecological environment but has also greatly constrained the local economy and tourism development.
